trajectory_summary: The call opened hostile and never recovered. Urban arrived with a resolved objection from a prior interaction; the rep's failure to establish legitimacy at the open, combined with reactive feature-explanation rather than emotional inversion, allowed Urban to re-entrench on each exchange. The call ended with an explicit removal request — not a soft hang-up, a procedural ask to be crossed off.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown' — the correct tag is 'no_appointment / DQ_by_customer_request.' Urban explicitly asked to be removed from contact ('you could just cross me off, please') at 02:05. This is not a callback, not a tentative, not a soft no — it is a hard exit initiated by the customer.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Objection inversion on quantified savings dismissal
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. I mean, isn't isn't that less than what your average bill is?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Urban handed the rep his exact bill number and the competing quote in the first 90 seconds. The rep's math-check accepted Urban's frame ($11 savings = not worth it) rather than inverting it. This was the earliest recoverable inflection point in the call — Urban was still talking, still explaining, still in the 'hostile-but-explaining' window. The inversion move (Urban's $138/month has no end date; the solar payment does) was available with Urban's own numbers and required no fabrication. Every subsequent exchange compounded this miss. Drill the inversion until it fires automatically when a customer quantifies a savings dismissal.

key_moments.stewart_read: Rep opens with a bare name-check — no company identification, no legitimacy framing, no reference to why the call is happening. The schema lists 'clear_company_introduction' as a required success signal for every intro variant. On a hostile customer who has already decided 'no,' the absence of legitimacy framing in the first sentence removes the only tool that could have interrupted Urban's autopilot before he restated his refusal.

Urban's opening line is a textbook hello_objection — a refusal in the first 30 seconds before any pitch has been made. The schema prescribes a specific procedure: acknowledge, then make a small ask ('Can I have just 30 seconds to tell you why I'm calling, and if it's not relevant, I'll leave you alone?'). The rep skipped this entirely and moved to diagnostic questioning ('What did they tell you?'). The diagnostic question happened to keep Urban talking, but it did so by entering Urban's frame rather than establishing a new one — a reactive recovery, not a protocol execution.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I mean, isn't isn't that less than what your average bill is?
key_moments.stewart_read: Urban has handed the rep his exact bill number ($138) and the competing quote ($127). The rep's response is a math-check — 'isn't that less?' — which is technically correct but emotionally flat. The schema's objection_inversion move was available here: flip Urban's $11 savings framing into the cost of staying ('What you can't afford is your current bill'). Instead the rep validated Urban's frame by accepting the $11 delta as the unit of analysis. Urban's response ('Yeah. $11. That that's not worth it to me.') confirms the frame was not interrupted.

key_moments.quote: Well, did you know that I don't know if you are aware, but solar actually has an end date. So once your panels are paid off, then you're you don't have an electric or a solar payment at all because you're just generating your own energy.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep pivots to a feature explanation — the payoff end-date — without anchoring it to Urban's stated concern. The schema flags 'explaining panel technology' and 'listing environmental benefits' as anti-patterns under curiosity_over_features; the end-date explanation is the same category of move. The rep is selling a feature rather than making Urban curious about his own specific math. The explanation also arrives after Urban has already re-entrenched (01:08), so it lands as a sales pitch rather than a discovery.
